Add tests that cover template loading, wizard generation, auto-placement, and rule-check behavior. These features will otherwise be easy to break as the app evolves.

Implementation checklist

Test wizard output for multiple scenarios.

Test rule checks for known good/bad plans.

Test sign manifest updates after auto-placement.

Add export regression tests for template-created plans.

Reset fields that should not carry over, such as dates or approvals.

Open duplicated plan as a new draft.

Add a test for deep copy correctness.

Validate plans against a first pass of MUTCD-style rules so users can catch issues before export. This should be a lightweight QC layer, not a full certification engine.

Define validation rules for taper length, sign spacing, and missing required devices.

Run checks whenever plan geometry changes.

Show warnings in a QC panel.

Link warnings to the affected object or geometry segment.

Add tests for valid and invalid layouts.

body:
Description
Ensure once status rules and locking are added, regressions are caught when editing/undo/redo.

Implementation checklist

Test scenarios

Plan transitions from DRAFT → SUBMITTED → APPROVED according to rules; illegal transitions fail.

In APPROVED or AS_BUILT, attempts to mutate plan (add/move/remove object, change metadata) fail at API level.

UI tests: in locked state, canvas tools/Save are disabled and “Create copy” works and opens an editable draft.

Undo/redo does nothing or is disabled in locked mode.

Implement as integration/system tests using your existing test framework (browser or headless canvas tests).

body:
Description
Add automated tests to ensure project permissions and share tokens are enforced correctly.

Implementation checklist

Test setup

Seed test users: owner, editor, viewer, outsider.

Seed a project with a plan and membership roles.

Scenarios

Owner/editor can read/write plan via authenticated APIs; viewer can read only; outsider is denied.

Owner/editor can create share link; viewer cannot.

/view/:token loads plan without auth, but invalid or revoked tokens fail.

Integrate into existing test framework (unit + system/e2e).

body:
Description
Prevent editing of plans once they are Approved/As‑built unless explicitly unlocked or duplicated.

Implementation checklist

Backend

Enforce editing restrictions in plan update APIs when status is APPROVED or AS_BUILT: reject write operations by default.

Provide POST /plans/{id}/duplicate endpoint to clone a plan (including geometry, metadata) back to DRAFT.

Frontend

If plan is locked:

Disable editing tools and show “Read‑only (Approved)” banner.

Provide “Create editable copy” button that calls duplicate endpoint and opens the new draft plan.

Ensure undo/redo is disabled or blocked in locked mode.

body:
Description
Create a public URL (e.g., /view/:token) that renders the plan canvas read‑only for agencies/clients.

Implementation checklist

Frontend

Add /view/:token route.

On mount, call GET /shares/{token} to get plan snapshot.

Render plan canvas in a locked mode:

No selection/move/add/remove tools.

No Save, no plan mutation calls.

Show top banner with project/plan name, status, and “Read only” indicator.

Backend

GET /shares/{token} should return only whitelisted fields necessary to render the plan (no sensitive user data).

body:
Description
Allow creating time‑revocable share tokens for plans, which back your read‑only public viewer.

Implementation checklist

Backend

Add PlanShareToken table: id, plan_id, token (random), created_at, created_by, expires_at (nullable), revoked_at (nullable), access_level (VIEW_ONLY).

Endpoint POST /plans/{id}/shares to create a token (optionally with expiration).

Endpoint GET /shares/{token} to fetch minimal plan data for viewer.

Endpoint DELETE /plans/{id}/shares/{shareId} to revoke.

Frontend

Represent a share record in TypeScript.

Use share records in the “Share plan” dialog (list, create, revoke).

body:
Description
Ensure plan access is governed by project membership/role and cannot be bypassed.

Implementation checklist

Backend

In plan read/update/delete endpoints, join to project and check ProjectMember for current user.

Define rules (example):

VIEWER: read‑only access.

EDITOR: read/write plan content but cannot delete project.

OWNER: full control, including deleting project or plans.

Add tests:

User with no membership cannot access a project plan.

Viewer cannot call update/delete.

Editor/owner can.

Frontend

Use role returned from project/plan API to disable editing controls in the UI for viewers (e.g., canvas interactions, Save button, toolbar actions).

Description
From a project, show all associated plans and support creating/attaching plans to that project.

Implementation checklist

Frontend

Add /projects/:projectId route.

Fetch project details and associated plans: GET /projects/{id} and/or GET /projects/{id}/plans.

Display project metadata and plans table (plan name, last modified, status).

“Create plan in this project” button that opens your existing “new plan” flow pre‑filled with projectId.

“Attach existing plan” modal: search/select from user plans not currently in a project, then call API to link.

Backend

Add GET /projects/{id}/plans endpoint.

Add API to attach/detach plan: POST /projects/{id}/plans/{planId}, DELETE /projects/{id}/plans/{planId}.

Description
Add role‑based membership so multiple users can collaborate on a project with different permissions.

Implementation checklist

Backend

Add ProjectMember table: project_id, user_id, role (OWNER, EDITOR, VIEWER), created_at.

Enforce: at least one OWNER per project.

Add endpoints:

GET /projects/{id}/members

POST /projects/{id}/members (add user + role)

PATCH /projects/{id}/members/{userId} (change role)

DELETE /projects/{id}/members/{userId}

Wire permission checks into project/plan APIs (e.g., only OWNER/EDITOR can modify).

Frontend

Add “Members” tab or panel on Project details page.

UI to add user by email/username and assign a role.

UI to change or remove roles (with safeguards around removing owners).

Introduce a Project model so multiple plans can be grouped under a named project (job, work order, client), with metadata and ownership. This is the backbone for collaboration and permissions.

Implementation checklist

Backend

Add Project table/model (e.g., id, name, description, created_at, updated_at, owner_user_id).

Add join table ProjectPlan (or project_id column on Plan) to associate plans with a project.

Implement CRUD endpoints: POST /projects, GET /projects, GET /projects/{id}, PATCH /projects/{id}, DELETE /projects/{id}.

Update plan load/save API to include project_id in responses/requests.

Frontend

Define TypeScript interfaces for Project and ProjectSummary.

Update plan state to include projectId.

Add project selector control when creating a new plan (or “No project” default).

body:
## Summary
Amplify Storage (S3) operations (`uploadData`, `list`, `getUrl`, `remove`) require AWS credentials. Currently `aws-exports.ts` only configures the Cognito User Pool — no Identity Pool is wired up.

Without an Identity Pool (or equivalent credentials provider), authenticated users will get credential errors when trying to save/load plans from S3, even if the bucket policy and User Pool are correctly configured.

## What needs to be done
1. Create a Cognito Identity Pool in AWS Console and link it to the User Pool
2. Add `aws_cognito_identity_pool_id` to `aws-exports.ts` and `VITE_COGNITO_IDENTITY_POOL_ID` to `.env.example`
3. Configure the Identity Pool's authenticated role to allow `s3:GetObject`, `s3:PutObject`, `s3:DeleteObject`, `s3:ListBucket` on `arn:aws:s3:::your-tcp-plans-bucket/plans/${cognito-identity.amazonaws.com:sub}/*`

## References
- Raised by Copilot on PR #75
- Related to issue #63 (S3 save/load implementation)
- Related to issue #69 (Activate Cognito auth for production)

body:
## Context
PR #68 added the full Amplify + Cognito auth layer. The frontend code is ready and bypasses auth in dev/CI when env vars are absent. This ticket covers the infra steps to flip it on in production.

## Tasks

- [ ] Create a Cognito User Pool in AWS Console (or via `amplify init && amplify add auth && amplify push`)
  - Email/password auth minimum
  - Enable self-registration or restrict to invited users (TBD)
- [ ] Copy the User Pool ID and App Client ID into the hosting environment
  - Add `VITE_AWS_REGION`, `VITE_COGNITO_USER_POOL_ID`, `VITE_COGNITO_APP_CLIENT_ID` as environment variables in Amplify Hosting / Vercel / wherever the frontend is deployed
  - Reference `.env.example` in `my-app/` for the exact variable names
- [ ] Verify the `<Authenticator>` sign-up/login wall appears on the production URL
- [ ] Verify sign-up confirmation email is delivered (SES sandbox may need approval for production volumes)
- [ ] Confirm `userId` is populated in downloaded `.tcp.json` plans after login

## Definition of Done
A user visiting the production URL sees the Cognito sign-up/login screen, can create an account, and the app recognises their unique ID.

## Notes
- No code changes required — all frontend wiring is in place
- See `my-app/src/aws-exports.ts` and `my-app/src/App.tsx` for implementation details

body:
## Summary

Configure domain routing so that **tcplanpro.com** is the primary domain, with **tcpmaker.com** and **trafficcontrolplanner.com** both issuing 301 redirects to it.

## Infrastructure

- Hosting: AWS Amplify
- DNS: AWS Route 53
- SSL: AWS ACM (Certificate Manager)

## Domains

| Domain | Role | Expiry |
|--------|------|--------|
| tcplanpro.com | Primary (Amplify custom domain) | March 22, 2027 |
| tcpmaker.com | Redirect → tcplanpro.com | March 22, 2027 |
| trafficcontrolplanner.com | Redirect → tcplanpro.com | March 22, 2027 |

## Architecture

Amplify's rewrite/redirect rules are path-based and cannot redirect across domains. The redirect domains must be handled outside Amplify:

```
tcpmaker.com          ──► Route 53 alias ──► CloudFront ──► S3 (website redirect to tcplanpro.com)
trafficcontrolplanner.com  ──► Route 53 alias ──► CloudFront ──► S3 (website redirect to tcplanpro.com)
tcplanpro.com         ──► Route 53 alias ──► Amplify (app)
```

## Implementation Steps

### 1. Add tcplanpro.com to Amplify
- Amplify Console → App → Domain management → Add domain → `tcplanpro.com`
- Map root and `www` subdomain to the production branch
- Amplify auto-provisions ACM cert and creates Route 53 records

### 2. For each redirect domain (tcpmaker.com, trafficcontrolplanner.com):

#### a. Create S3 bucket (website redirect)
- Bucket name must match the domain (e.g. `tcpmaker.com`)
- Enable static website hosting
- Set redirect: all requests → `tcplanpro.com`, protocol `https`
- No bucket policy or content needed

#### b. Request ACM certificate
- ACM Console (us-east-1 — required for CloudFront) → Request public cert
- Add both `tcpmaker.com` and `www.tcpmaker.com`
- Validate via DNS (ACM will offer to create Route 53 records automatically)

#### c. Create CloudFront distribution
- Origin: S3 website endpoint (not the REST endpoint)
- Viewer protocol: Redirect HTTP to HTTPS
- Alternate domain names (CNAMEs): `tcpmaker.com`, `www.tcpmaker.com`
- SSL cert: the ACM cert from step b
- Default cache behaviour: no caching needed (redirect is instant)

#### d. Update Route 53
- Hosted zone for `tcpmaker.com`
- A record (alias) → CloudFront distribution
- AAAA record (alias) → same CloudFront distribution
- Repeat for `www.tcpmaker.com`

### 3. Verify
- `curl -I https://tcpmaker.com` → `301` with `Location: https://tcplanpro.com`
- `curl -I https://trafficcontrolplanner.com/some/path` → `301` to `https://tcplanpro.com` (note: S3 redirect does not preserve path; use CloudFront Function if path preservation is needed)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] tcplanpro.com serves the Amplify app over HTTPS
- [ ] tcpmaker.com → 301 → tcplanpro.com
- [ ] www.tcpmaker.com → 301 → tcplanpro.com
- [ ] trafficcontrolplanner.com → 301 → tcplanpro.com
- [ ] www.trafficcontrolplanner.com → 301 → tcplanpro.com
- [ ] All domains have valid SSL (no browser warnings)

## Notes

- ACM certs for CloudFront **must** be requested in **us-east-1** regardless of your app's region
- S3 bucket website redirect does not preserve the URL path. If path preservation matters, replace the S3 redirect with a CloudFront Function (two-line JS, free tier)
- Both redirect domains follow identical steps — do one first, then repeat for the second

body:
Enhance intersection handling and road connection logic.

## Requirements (from #65)

- Extend `snapToEndpoint()` to support snapping to any point on a road segment, not just endpoints
- Create a T-Junction and 4-Way template tool that generates pre-aligned road segments
- Support connecting multiple road types (e.g. 2-lane road meeting a 4-lane highway) without visual gaps

## Original scope
- Intersection snapping
- Merge lanes
- Crossing geometry

## Definition of Done
Users can connect multiple road types at intersections without visual gaps, and snap to any point on a road segment (not just endpoints).
